key_insight: Radial band-gap collapse around a swift heavy ion creates a transient metal-semiconductor structure in Al2O3 that changes how electronic energy is transferred to the lattice and how ion tracks form.
engineering_relevance: Track-damage predictions for irradiation-resistant ceramics should account for spatially varying electronic properties near the ion path, not just uniform energy deposition.
method: TREKIS Monte Carlo simulates initial electronic and ionic excitations after ion passage.
DFT-based molecular dynamics tracks band-structure changes inside the ion track.
The resulting radial band-gap profile is used to estimate local atomic heating and damage formation.
evidence_signal: For a 700 MeV Bi ion, the abstract reports metallization within ~2 nm of the trajectory, band-gap shrinkage at ~3–5 nm, and negligible change beyond 5 nm, leading to more discontinuous damage patterns than a uniform-deposition model.
watch_item: The approach depends on coupled multiscale modeling assumptions and material-specific band-gap evolution, so transferability to other ions, energies, or ceramics may require recalibration.
paper_title: Effect of radially heterogeneous band gap collapse on formation of swift heavy ion tracks in Al2O3
